content.text: are standardized processes that teams follow to manage code changes collaboratively using Git. They define how developers create
before merging changes into the main codebase. The core workflow is:
create a branch → make changes → push to remote → create PR → get review → merge

Without a structured Git workflow:
- Code changes collide, causing merge conflicts
- Bugs slip into production without review
- Team coordination breaks down
- Repository history becomes chaotic and hard to debug
Feature branches prevent breaking main code
Code review catches bugs before merge
Organized commits and branches make debugging easier
Regular pulls from main reduce merge issues
Review process adds time before merging
Developers may get overwhelmed by many reviews
Merge conflicts still require manual fixing

1. [Developer] Clone/pull source of work target
2. [Developer] Create branch for work
3. [Developer] Add/modify functions (development work)
4. [Developer] Push completed task to remote
5. [Developer] Create PR (specify merge branch → target branch)
6. [Reviewer] Check changes and review
7. [Reviewer] Send feedback if necessary
8. [Reviewer] Merge if no problems
9. [Reviewer] Close if PR becomes unnecessary
Repeat steps 3–7 as needed to improve code quality.

# Start from develop/main branch
git checkout develop

# Create and switch to new feature branch
git checkout -b feature/something
Feature branches isolate changes, preventing work from breaking the main codebase.

# Stage changes
git add .

# Commit with meaningful message
git commit -m "Implement something feature"
This shares your changes with the team and prepares for PR creation.

On GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket:
1. Navigate to repository
2. Switch to your branch
3. Click "Pull requests" → "New pull request"
4. Select

(your feature)
5. Review changes that will be merged
6. Add

explaining what changes do and why
7. Assign
On GitHub, a PR is a "reviewable set of changes" — you're requesting maintainers to "pull" your suggested changes into the project.

:
1. Go to the PR on GitHub
2. Click "Files changed" tab to see modifications
3. Leave

next to problematic lines
4. Discuss issues/suggestions directly within PR
5.

:
- Don't just use GitHub's generated diff —
pull the branch and test yourself

- Set specific times to check PRs
- Add additional edits/commits if it saves back-and-forth

# Make changes locally on your feature branch
git checkout feature/something

# Implement fixes, commit, push
git add .
git commit -m "Fix: Address reviewer feedback"
git push origin feature/something

Once approved and all issues resolved:
1. Click "Merge pull request" button on PR page
2. Confirm merge
3. Optionally
: Wait for automated tests (CI) to ensure changes don't break the codebase.
: Don't bypass review—even for small changes, maintain code quality.
Each feature in isolated branch → merge to main

# Fetch latest branches
git fetch

# Pull latest main changes
git checkout master
git pull origin master

# Return to branch and merge master
git checkout my_new_branch
git merge master

# Resolve conflicts in editor (IDEs assist)
git commit -am "Merging master into my_new_branch"
git push origin my_new_branch
Then retry the PR on GitHub UI.
when not ready for review (won't auto-request reviewers)

# 1. Start from develop
git checkout develop

# 2. Create feature branch
git checkout -b feature/user-auth

# 3. Make changes
git add .
git commit -m "Add JWT authentication"

# 4. Push
git push -u origin feature/user-auth

# 5. Create PR on GitHub → merge to develop
# 6. Reviewer approves → merge
# 7. Delete branch

Developer creates PR
  ↓
Reviewer comments: "Use more exclamation points!!!" [web:11]
  ↓
Developer makes fix commit
  ↓
Pushes → PR updates automatically [web:11]
  ↓
Reviewer approves → merge
Team of 10 developers at mid-sized company

: Automated tests run on every PR
-
Hard to review, high conflict risk
: Pull branch and run before approving
GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, CircleCI (auto-test PRs)
Feature Branch + PR is industry standard

:
- Unreviewed code → security vulnerabilities
- Merging broken code → production failures
- Accidental main branch changes

: No direct commits, must use PR
-
for WIP to avoid premature review requests

Git workflows stay fast at scale by enforcing discipline around branch lifetime, PR size, and CI gating. The two variables that most affect team throughput are pull request size and the number of review cycles.
Reviewers miss bugs; review time grows super-linearly with diff size
Long-lived branches accumulate merge conflicts; integration risk rises
Developers bypass CI or merge without waiting; defect rate rises
> 3 rounds signals unclear requirements or insufficient PR description
More branches increase context switching and rebase maintenance cost
A broken main blocks every developer; enforce branch protection rules

For monorepos with many packages, scope CI to changed packages using path filters (GitHub Actions paths:, GitLab only: changes) so unrelated tests do not run on every PR. Use shallow clones (git clone --depth 1) in CI to reduce checkout time from minutes to seconds for large repositories. Git LFS (Large File Storage) prevents repository bloat from binary assets — without it, a 50 MB design asset committed to history permanently inflates every future clone.
Merge conflicts rise with branch lifetime and with the number of developers modifying the same files. Three practices prevent conflict accumulation:
Rebase from main daily. A daily git fetch origin && git rebase origin/main keeps your branch at most one day of divergence, making conflicts small and localized.
Decompose large features into stacked PRs. Each PR builds on the previous merged one. This keeps individual diffs under 400 lines and allows incremental review.

Assign ownership of high-churn files. Use CODEOWNERS to route PRs touching shared configuration or core modules to the team member who owns that area. Fewer simultaneous edits to the same file means fewer conflicts.
Git stores file contents as content-addressed blobs: each unique file state is stored once and referenced by its SHA-1 hash. This means

— a branch is just a 41-byte file pointing to a commit hash. Switching branches is also fast: git checkout updates the working tree only for files that differ between commits. The cost of a branch operation scales with the size of the
, not the size of the repository.

git gc (garbage collection) periodically packs loose objects into pack files and removes unreachable objects. For active repositories this runs automatically. On very large monorepos (hundreds of thousands of commits), git maintenance --auto with a scheduled pack task keeps the object store compact and fetch times low.

# Enable background maintenance for large repos
git maintenance start
# Runs: gc, commit-graph, fetch, loose-objects, incremental-repack on schedule

# Shallow clone for CI — fetches only the last 50 commits
git clone --depth 50 https://github.com/org/repo.git

# Partial clone — skip blobs, fetch on demand (for repos with large binaries)
git clone --filter=blob:none https://github.com/org/repo.git

# Measure repository size and top-contributing objects
git count-objects -vH
git rev-list --objects --all | sort -k2 | cut -f2 | uniq -d | head -20
Git Object Model and Commit Graph

Git's data model is a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of immutable content-addressed objects. Understanding this model clarifies why branches are cheap, rebases are safe, and merge conflicts arise where they do.
Raw file content, addressed by SHA-1 hash of its contents. Two commits referencing the same file version share one blob — no duplication.
A directory snapshot: a list of (mode, name, hash) tuples pointing to blobs and other trees. Each commit captures exactly one root tree.

Points to a root tree, stores author/committer metadata and timestamp, and lists one or more parent commit hashes. The parent list is what makes git history a graph: most commits have one parent; merge commits have two.
A named pointer to a specific commit, with a GPG signature. Used for releases.
A branch is simply a mutable pointer (a 41-byte file in
) to the most recent commit on that branch. Creating a branch is O(1) regardless of history length.
is a pointer to the current branch (or commit in detached HEAD state).
Both merge and rebase integrate changes from one branch into another, but they produce different graph shapes:
Creates a new merge commit with two parents
Non-linear; preserves exact history of when branches diverged
Public branches, shared history, permanent record of integration point
Replays feature commits on top of main tip, rewriting SHA hashes
Linear; looks as if feature was developed after main's current tip
Private/local branches before opening a PR; keeps main history clean
Collapses all feature commits into one staged change (no merge commit)
Clean-up PRs where intermediate commits are not meaningful

a first-class debugging tool: it shows merge commits as forks, rebase histories as straight lines, and divergence points visually. Understanding the graph prevents the common mistake of rebasing public (shared) branches — rebase rewrites commit hashes, so any collaborator who based work on the original commits will have divergent history.

# Visualize the commit graph for the last 20 commits
git log --graph --oneline --decorate --all -n 20

# Example output:
# * a3f9c21 (HEAD -> feature/auth) Add OAuth token refresh
# * 7d1b4e8 Add OAuth login handler
# | * c82a3d1 (main) Fix typo in README
# |/
# * 4e9b2f0 Initial auth scaffold
# * 8c3d1a2 (origin/main) Release v1.2.0

# Three-way merge: find the common ancestor of feature and main
git merge-base feature/auth main  # returns base commit hash

# Rebase feature onto current main (rewrites feature commits)
git checkout feature/auth
git rebase main
# If conflict on a specific commit:
# git rebase --continue   (after resolving conflict)
# git rebase --abort      (cancel rebase entirely)

# Squash all feature commits into one before merging
git checkout main
git merge --squash feature/auth
git commit -m "feat: add OAuth login and token refresh"
GitFlow formalizes the branch types used in versioned software releases. Each branch type has a specific source, target, and naming convention:
Production-ready code; every commit is a deployable release
Integration branch; all feature work targets here
Individual feature or bugfix in development
Stabilization branch for a release candidate; only bugfixes
Urgent production fix bypassing the release cycle

# GitFlow initialization (using git-flow CLI tool)
git flow init
# Sets branch names, prefixes for feature/release/hotfix

# Start a new feature
git flow feature start TICKET-42-add-payment

# Finish a feature (merges to develop, deletes feature branch)
git flow feature finish TICKET-42-add-payment

# Start a release candidate
git flow release start v2.1.0

# Finish a release (merges to main + develop, creates tag)
git flow release finish v2.1.0

# Emergency hotfix on production
git flow hotfix start v2.0.1
# ... fix the bug ...
git flow hotfix finish v2.0.1
# Merges to main + develop, creates tag v2.0.1
Conventional Commits is a specification for commit message format that enables automated changelog generation, semantic versioning, and better git log readability. The format is:

Where type is one of: feat (new feature), fix (bug fix), docs (documentation), style (formatting), refactor (no behavior change), test (add/fix tests), chore (build/tooling). A BREAKING CHANGE footer or ! after type triggers a major version bump in automated tooling.

# Good conventional commit messages:
git commit -m "feat(auth): add OAuth2 PKCE flow for mobile clients"
git commit -m "fix(api): handle null response from payment gateway"
git commit -m "feat!: remove deprecated v1 API endpoints

BREAKING CHANGE: All v1 endpoints removed. Clients must migrate to v2."

# Bad commit messages (non-informative):
git commit -m "fix stuff"
git commit -m "WIP"
git commit -m "asdfgh"

# Auto-generate CHANGELOG from conventional commits:
# npx conventional-changelog-cli -p angular -i CHANGELOG.md -s
Git workflows impose a discipline of branch → commit → push → PR → review → merge. The branch is the unit of work; the PR is the unit of collaboration.
Feature branches isolate every change from main. Nothing lands in main without a PR — this keeps main always deployable and creates a traceable audit trail.
Pull request size is the single biggest driver of review quality. PRs under 400 lines receive 2× more meaningful comments than PRs over 1,000 lines. Keep them small and single-purpose.

CI must be a hard gate. A merged PR that breaks main costs every developer on the team. Branch protection rules (require status checks, require reviews, no direct push to main) enforce this automatically.
Rebasing from main daily prevents merge conflict accumulation. A 24-hour-old branch has manageable conflicts; a two-week-old branch has compounding ones.

Choose your workflow by team size and release cadence: GitHub Flow (branch + PR) for continuous delivery teams; GitFlow for versioned release products; trunk-based development for large teams with comprehensive feature flags.
Git branch operations are O(1) — branching is free. The cost comes from diff size at merge time, not from the number of branches. Use branches liberally but merge them quickly.
